texts.full_text: initial presentation of signet-ring cell carcinoma of the colon. I here report a caseA 63-year-old male presented to the clinic with a two-month history of recurrent wheals distributed over the trunk and extremities. The patient had no previous medical history, but routine laboratory evaluation at the time of admission revealed an elevated white blood cell count (15.3 × 109/L) and elevated lactate dehydrogenase level (527 U/L). Serum carcinoembryonic antigen level (195.5 ng/mL) and cancer antigen 19-9 (77 U/mL) were also elevated. Skin biopsy demonstrated leukocytoclastic vasculitis without evidence of immune complex deposition. Extensive work-up failed to reveal the underlying etiology until colonoscopy was performed. Colonic biopsy showed signet-ring cell carcinoma. After resection of the tumor, the patient’s vasculitis resolved completely without further treatment. Urticarial vasculitis (UV) is a rare cutaneous vasculitis characterized by burning, stinging, and recurrent wheals on the skin. It is a type of hypersensitivity vasculitis, which is frequently idiopath eremitting, but may be associated with underlying diseases such as systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), Sjögren’s syndrome, and chronic infections. However, paraneoplastic vasculitis as a manifestation of underlying malignancy has been described in the literature. Malignancy-associated UV, particularly associated with colonic adenocarcinoma, has been scarcely reported. This case report highlights the importance of considering malignancy in the work-up of UV, especially in older patients with an atypical presentation
